content:
Hi,

I was wondering if there is some place where the wisdom acquired by the community about the correct way to reference C data structures in the kernel has been collected.

Considering the subtle differences between *T, NonNull<T>, Opaque<T> and &mut (among other issues), I feel I can't yet fully grasp which is the correct choice in which place when creating wrappers.

Like e.g. is Opaque<T> needed instead of an immutable & if the contract says the target won't be modified during the existence of the ref? Or like, can a & always be created if the target is valid by C standards (it might still contain uninitialized fields)?

I hope I'm not the only one who can't wrap his head around the matter instantly :)
The default should be to make a Rust struct wrapping an Opaque<bindings::c_type>, and use &_ and ARef<_> to reference it.
Opaque prevents the compiler from making assumptions about the data it contains. The type documentation has some details.

Oliver Mangold said:

I hope I'm not the only one who can't wrap his head around the matter instantly :)

Don't worry, you are not. I always felt like a single point of reference documentation all things required to work with rust in the kernel has been missing. Unfortunately nobody has had enough spare cycles to writhe it yet. The rust nomicon is a good resource as a whole and in particular the FFI section is relevant.

Andreas Hindborg said:

I always felt like a single point of reference documentation all things required to work with rust in the kernel has been missing. Unfortunately nobody has had enough spare cycles to writhe it yet.

Yes. Some weeks ago there was the idea to do something like a "User guide for kernel C developers moving to Rust for Linux" (or similar :wink: ).

Even if nobody has time to write it, maybe we could at least discuss and agree on a format and place to do something like this? And create a starting point? And make it easy and fast to add something (e.g. < 5 - 10min?) so that somebody who as something in mind (like discussed here) can quickly add it? I always like a wiki for this kind of stuff, but most probably there are better alternatives?

I can tell what experience we had at work, where we also have need for documentation of a similar kind. We started out with Mediawiki, which is quite pleasant to work with, at least at the beginning, as it has lots of formatting options and plugins for that purpose. Efficient to edit, as it is almost WYSIWYG and doesn't need anything but a browser.

But after a couple of years parts of the crew became unhappy with it, because it is a not particularly convenient to track changes. Thus we moved to documenting in RST, putting the files in a repo in Gitlab and make the Gitlab CI auto-rebuild these to html which is published through Gitlab Pages. Certainly less messy, but you need Git and Sphinx locally installed and run a rebuild to see how the change looks.

Not sure what would be better, but an argument in favor of RST might also be, that when parts of the docs are stabilized they could be upstreamed into the main kernel documentation.
I think mdbook is the obvious choice for something version controlled and rust adjacent. It's widely used in the rust community.
Yes, please choose markdown, sphinx is absolutely horrendous wrt "compile" times.
We have a kernel::ffi module now. Seems like a reasonable place to put it.

Benno Lossin said:

please choose markdown

Andreas Hindborg said:

I think mdbook is the obvious choice for something version controlled and rust adjacent.

Does anybody have a proposal where would be good place to give either of these options a try? Something like free mdbook or markdown hosting space?

Does anybody have a proposal where would be good place to give either of these options a try? Something like free mdbook or markdown hosting space?

I didn't test it, but according to this:

https://docs.gitlab.com/ee/user/project/pages/introduction.html

the public Gitlab instance would host and build that for free.

You have to create a container configured to do the build, but that isn't hard.

Yes, Gitlab or Github CI is also was the mdbook projects suggests:

https://rust-lang.github.io/mdBook/continuous-integration.html

Dirk Behme said:

Does anybody have a proposal where would be good place to give either of these options a try? Something like free mdbook or markdown hosting space?

mdBook is easy to run (and build IIRC, but there are pre-built releases you can download), so you can try locally very quickly. https://rust-for-linux.com is a (customized) mdBook, in fact! :)
Maybe today's posting Rust for Linux curriculum for Comprehensive Rust could be a place where we could document what we discussed above?

Dirk Behme said:

Maybe today's posting Rust for Linux curriculum for Comprehensive Rust could be a place where we could document what we discussed above?

I discuss this a little here; hopefully what I have there is an accurate description.
